findings.title: User-controlled host concatenated into shell command
findings.description: runPing concatenates the untrusted `host` argument directly into a shell string passed to a shell-exec call. An attacker who controls `host` can inject arbitrary shell metacharacters (e.g. `8.8.8.8; rm -rf /`) and run commands.
findings.exploit_scenario: An HTTP handler calls runPing(req.query.host); attacker sends ?host=8.8.8.8;id and the injected `id` runs.
findings.recommendation: Use execFile with an argv array, or validate host against a strict IP/hostname allowlist before interpolation.

findings.title: Shell injection in ping helper
findings.description: The ping helper builds a command string from caller input and runs it via a shell. Same root cause as the command-injection finding above; reported by a second scanner with different wording.
findings.recommendation: Avoid the shell; pass arguments as an array.

findings.title: Username concatenated into SQL query
findings.description: lookupUser builds a SQL string by concatenating the untrusted `username` argument directly into the WHERE clause, with no parameterization or escaping. Classic SQL injection.
findings.exploit_scenario: username = "' OR '1'='1" returns every row; a UNION payload exfiltrates other tables.
findings.recommendation: Use a parameterized query / prepared statement with bound parameters.

findings.title: Math.random used for a value flagged as a security token
findings.description: The scanner flagged the buffer index computed with Math.random at line 33 as a predictable security token. Re-reading the code shows the value is only an index into a read-only sample buffer for a demo animation; it is never used as a token, secret, or identifier.
findings.recommendation: Use a CSPRNG for security tokens.

findings.title: Possible null dereference on config object
findings.description: The scanner claims getConfigValue dereferences `config.value` without a null check. Re-reading shows an explicit `if (!config) return undefined` guard at line 44 immediately before the dereference, so the path the scanner traced is already handled.
